title: Dog Whining and Looking at Flank: Common Causes and Care Steps
meta_desc: Is your dog whining and looking at their flank? Learn what this behavior means, the urgency level, and how to help your pet find relief.
content_html:
TL;DR: A dog looking at their flank and whining typically indicates localized pain or internal distress, ranging from muscle strain to serious conditions like bloat or kidney issues. Seek emergency care immediately if your dog has a distended abdomen, is trying to vomit unsuccessfully, or shows extreme lethargy.

What does it mean if my dog keeps looking at their flank and whining?

  • Looking at, nuzzling, or biting the flank—the fleshy area between the ribs and the hind legs—is usually a localized sign of discomfort or pain.
  • This behavior is often your dog's way of pointing to the source of a physical sensation they cannot resolve.
  • It may be accompanied by whining, restlessness, or an inability to get comfortable.

Is my dog looking at their flank and whining a veterinary emergency?

  • The urgency level for flank watching and whining is Medium.
  • While it can be caused by minor skin irritations or muscle strain, it is also a classic sign of internal issues like kidney pain, gastrointestinal distress, or early-stage bloat (GDV).
  • Treat as a high-priority emergency if your dog has a distended abdomen, is attempting to vomit without success, or shows extreme lethargy.
  • Otherwise, contact your veterinarian for a same-day or next-day appointment to rule out serious internal complications.

How does sending a photo or video help my veterinarian triage the issue?

  • A photo of the flank area allows the vet to check for external clues like rashes, insect bites, or swelling.
  • A video of the whining and the way your dog moves helps the professional gauge the severity of the pain.
  • These visuals help determine if the issue is neurological, musculoskeletal, or internal.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In dogs, whining and flank-watching can indicate several underlying conditions. Ureteral obstruction due to urolithiasis may cause vomiting, lethargy, and flank or renal pain, especially with acute, total obstruction and renal capsule distention. Renal inflammation and capsular swelling can also lead to flank pain, and occasionally vomiting; however, early signs of renal dysfunction are typically polydipsia and polyuria. In intact male dogs, prostatic diseases, particularly acute bacterial infections, abscesses, or neoplasia, can cause caudal abdominal pain, stiffness, fever, or malaise, potentially manifesting as discomfort in the flank area.

Chapter: Nephrology, Internal Medicine

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1514)

faqs.question: Could my dog be experiencing bloat?
faqs.answer: Yes, looking at the flank is a common sign of abdominal pain associated with bloat. Watch for a hard tummy and unproductive retching.

faqs.question: Can fleas or skin allergies cause this?
faqs.answer: Yes, intense itching or a sudden bite in the flank area can cause a dog to snap their head around and whine in frustration.

faqs.question: Should I give my dog human pain relievers?
faqs.answer: No. Never give human medications like ibuprofen or aspirin to a dog, as these can be toxic. Wait for professional veterinary guidance.
